myths.Agent_1:
Long ago, before the sky had stars, the world was blanketed in an endless, velvet darkness. The people relied solely on the Moon, a great silver moth that fluttered across the heavens, to guide their nocturnal hunts. But the Moon was fickle, often hiding its face behind thick, woolen clouds.

A young weaver named Elara decided to craft a celestial tapestry to guide her people. She spent her days gathering drops of morning dew, capturing the pure sunlight within them before dusk fell. Using the strongest strands of mountain spider silk, she carefully wove these glowing droplets into a massive, glittering net. 

When the Moon vanished during a brutal winter night, Elara climbed to the highest peak, carrying her luminous creation. With a mighty heave, she cast the net high into the sky. The spider silk dissolved into the ether, but the glowing dewdrops remained, permanently scattered across the dark canvas. They became the first stars, steadfast and unblinking. 

To this day, the stars form intricate patterns—the remnants of Elara’s woven net. And when the morning dew settles on the grass, it is said to be the stars' reflection, honoring the weaver who gave the night sky its eternal light.
myths.Agent_2:
Long ago, before the sky had a voice, the clouds were heavy, silent beasts that wandered the heavens. They drank from the oceans but never gave the water back, causing the earth below to crack and wither. The animals pleaded with the Sun, but the Sun only scorched the ground further, too proud to ever hide his radiant face behind the selfish clouds.

A small, incredibly clever sparrow named Lira decided to finally act. She flew as high as her little wings could carry her, clutching a single, sharp thorn from the silver-bramble bush. When she reached the belly of the darkest, most bloated cloud, she struck. She dragged the long thorn across its soft, dark underbelly.

The cloud let out a magnificent, booming roar—the very first thunder. From the wound, bright glowing sparks erupted—the first lightning. And then, the wounded cloud began to weep. Cool, life-giving rain poured from the sky, healing the fractured earth.

To this very day, the clouds rumble and flash in memory of the sparrow’s brave strike. And whenever the storms gather, the sparrows fly low to the ground, knowing well that their ancestor's tiny thorn is what taught the sky how to cry, and the earth how to bloom.

Myth: After the brave sparrow Lira pierced the heavy clouds to bring the first rain, the sky wept for forty days and forty nights. The earth, once parched, was now drowning under the sorrow of the wounded heavens. The thunder roared endlessly, and the jagged lightning scarred the darkened sky. 

A humble mountain spider named Kaelen saw that the world needed a reprieve. He knew the clouds were too high for him to reach, so he waited on the highest peak for the lightning to strike. When a brilliant bolt shattered a nearby ancient pine, Kaelen moved quickly. He gathered the lingering, colorful sparks of the shattered wood—red from the bark, gold from the sap, blue from the rain, and violet from the twilight. 

Using his strongest, most delicate silk, Kaelen wove these glowing fragments together into a massive, shimmering ribbon. He cast his colorful web into the wind, and it caught on the retreating edges of the storm. The giant ribbon stretched across the sky, bridging the earth and the heavens. 

The weeping clouds saw the beautiful creation and finally smiled, their tears slowing to a gentle mist. Thus, the first rainbow was born, a woven promise of peace after the storm.

The sky was healed, but the vibrant stitches remained. Now, on the coldest, clearest nights, you can still see Caelen’s handiwork shimmering across the polar skies. The Northern Lights dance and ripple—a breathtaking, glowing scar that reminds the world of the weaver who stitched the heavens whole.

Myth: When Caelen finished stitching the weeping heavens to create the Northern Lights, not all of his vibrant sunset threads remained in the sky. As the great silver Moon-moth descended, a few stray strands of deep crimson, bright gold, and bruised violet slipped from the saddle and drifted down to the silent, shivering forests below.

A swift and curious fox named Ignis discovered these glowing threads resting on the frost-covered moss. Winter was rapidly approaching, and the green canopy was already turning dull and gray, leaving the woodland creatures bracing for the bitter chill. Ignis, wishing to capture the warmth of the sunset threads, took them gently in his jaws. 

He darted through the woods, weaving the fallen celestial silk among the branches of the great oaks and maples. Wherever the fox passed, the dull leaves ignited with brilliant colors, absorbing the lingering magic of the sky-weaver’s thread. 

The trees shed these beautiful leaves to blanket the forest floor, protecting the sleeping earth from the frost. To this day, when autumn arrives, the forests burst into shades of crimson, gold, and violet—a fleeting reflection of the Northern Lights, gifted to the earth by a running fox.

Before the next storm could completely fade, Thorne scurried to the very edge of the valley where the shimmering ribbon touched the earth. With his sturdy claws, he carefully scraped the dying embers of colored light—ruby reds, golden yellows, and deep blues—and buried them safely beneath the damp soil to protect them from the night.

Through the long, dark winter, Thorne guarded the buried light. When the soil finally warmed, the earth did not just thaw; it erupted in color. From the spots where Thorne had planted the rainbow’s remains, delicate stalks pushed through the dirt, unfurling petals of crimson, gold, and violet. 

These were the world’s first flowers. Now, even when the sky is gray and the rainbow is hidden, the earth remembers the storm’s woven promise through the vibrant, blooming fields below.
